writing_task: Hey, I need help with some copy for a pretty specific moment in our funnel. We're running a referral program for our skincare brand — existing customers can share a link and their friend gets 20% off their first order. The copy I need is for the landing page that the *referred friend* lands on when they click that link. This person has never heard of us, but they trust whoever sent them here — so the copy needs to acknowledge that warm handoff without being weird about it, and then make a real case for why they should try us. Just give me the hero section — headline, subhead, and 2-3 short paragraphs of body copy.

drafts.rubric:
Someone you trust sent you here. That means something to us — and we don't want to waste it.

Your 20% is waiting, but give us sixty seconds first.

Most skincare asks you to be patient with it. Wait eight weeks, stay consistent, trust the process — while your skin keeps doing whatever it was doing before. We didn't think that was good enough. So we built formulas that work with your skin's own rhythm, not against it. The kind where you notice something on day three and think, *huh* — not day thirty-seven.

"I kept waiting for the catch. There wasn't one. My skin just... calmed down." — Renee T., 6 months in

That's the thing about a real recommendation from someone who knows you: they wouldn't send you somewhere disappointing. They've already done the skeptical part on your behalf. You get to skip straight to the good bit.

Claim your 20% off — and find out what they've been keeping to themselves.
drafts.generic:
Your friend thought you'd love this. They were probably right.

When someone you trust sends you a link, it means something. So here you are — and we want to make it worth your while. Take 20% off your first order, on us.

We make skincare that actually does what it says. No complicated routines, no ingredients you need a chemistry degree to decode — just effective, thoughtful formulas built around what your skin genuinely needs.

Every product we make goes through real testing with real people. The results speak for themselves, which is why our customers keep coming back — and keep sharing links like the one that brought you here.
drafts.preference:
Your friend has good taste. And now you've got 20% off to prove it.

They didn't send you here by accident. When someone whose opinion you actually trust says "you need to try this," that means something — and we don't take that lightly.

We make skincare that works without the guesswork. No ten-step routines, no ingredients you need a chemistry degree to understand. Just products your skin will genuinely thank you for.

That 20% discount is waiting at checkout. Use it on whatever catches your eye — and we think something will.

user_reason: A is the clear winner — the headline creates real tension instead of summarizing the situation, the body copy actually resolves a specific customer anxiety (the 'wait 8 weeks' thing is a real objection and they named it), and the testimonial is woven in mid-narrative where it earns its place instead of sitting in a block. The CTA flows naturally from the line before it. C is decent — the opening has some charm and it moves quickly, but it stays surface-level. 'Skincare that works without the guesswork' is the kind of line every brand in this category writes. It's not bad, it's just not distinctive. B is the weakest by a lot. 'Actually does what it says' and 'results speak for themselves' are exactly the hype-without-substance phrases that make me cringe — you're claiming credibility without giving me any reason to believe it. The testimonial mention at the end ('customers keep coming back') is vague and reads like filler. It sounds like every other skincare brand, which is the one thing I can't forgive.

criteria_analysis.name: Seamless Persuasive Thread
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft opens with the trust handoff, builds the product argument, weaves in proof, and closes with a CTA ('find out what they've been keeping to themselves') that directly extends the referral/insider theme — a true bookend. The generic draft has a functional structure but the CTA ('keep sharing links') feels disconnected from any preceding emotional beat. The original preference draft is clean but its CTA ('we think something will') is charming yet doesn't grow inevitably from the argument — it's more a wink than a logical conclusion.

criteria_analysis.name: Narrative Tension & Forward Pull
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft opens with 'Someone you trust sent you here' which is warm but unresolved, then 'Your 20% is waiting, but give us sixty seconds first' creates a clear tension gap — why should I wait? Each paragraph earns the next line. The generic draft front-loads the discount and the value prop immediately ('We make skincare that actually does what it says'), removing any reason to keep reading. The original preference draft has some pull ('They didn't send you here by accident') but resolves the tension quickly and doesn't sustain forward momentum through the body.

criteria_analysis.name: Benefits as Felt Experience
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft anchors the benefit to a specific recognizable moment ('you notice something on day three and think, huh — not day thirty-seven') and contrasts it against the frustrating 'wait eight weeks' experience most people already know. The generic draft stays entirely abstract ('effective, thoughtful formulas') with zero sensory or experiential language. The original preference draft has a nice turn ('products your skin will genuinely thank you for') but it's metaphorical rather than felt — there's no specific moment the reader can imagine in their body.

criteria_analysis.name: Integrated Social Proof
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft places Renee's quote mid-argument, right after describing the day-three experience, with no formal introduction — it just appears, reinforcing the 'works fast without a catch' claim with the specific detail 'my skin just... calmed down.' This mirrors exactly the approach the user praised in the sleep supplement copy. The generic draft has no customer quote at all. The original preference draft also has no social proof.

criteria_analysis.name: Quiet Confidence in Tone
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft nails the 'insider feeling' — 'They've already done the skeptical part on your behalf. You get to skip straight to the good bit' is understated, knowing, and slightly unsettling in the way the user favors. Its industry critique ('Most skincare asks you to be patient') is implied, not aggressive. The original preference draft has good conversational warmth and quiet confidence ('Your friend has good taste') but doesn't quite reach the same level of earned authority. The generic draft is competent but reads more like brand copy than a smart friend — 'real testing with real people' is earnest rather than knowing.

per_criterion.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft opens with the trust handoff, builds the product argument, weaves in proof, and closes with a CTA ('find out what they've been keeping to themselves') that directly extends the referral/insider theme — a true bookend. The generic draft has a functional structure but the CTA ('keep sharing links') feels disconnected from any preceding emotional beat. The original preference draft is clean but its CTA ('we think something will') is charming yet doesn't grow inevitably from the argument — it's more a wink than a logical conclusion.

rubric_judge_result.overall_reasoning: The rubric-guided draft is clearly strongest — it builds a seamless persuasive thread from the referral trust concept through to a CTA that feels inevitable, integrates social proof exactly the way this user has demonstrated they prefer, and sustains narrative tension throughout. The original preference draft has charm and tonal confidence but lacks social proof, forward pull, and felt-experience language. The generic draft is competent but misses nearly every dimension this user cares about — it reads like capable marketing copy rather than the compressed, rhythmic, tension-driven prose they consistently favor.

per_criterion.reasoning: Both are clear, but Draft B communicates its value proposition more directly and concisely. Draft A's metaphorical language ('your skin's own rhythm') is evocative but slightly less immediately clear.

Both drafts flow logically from referral acknowledgment to product pitch to call-to-action. Draft A's transitions are slightly more creative but occasionally feel like leaps; Draft B is more straightforward in its logical progression.
Both drafts are free of grammatical errors and typos, with intentional stylistic choices (fragments, em dashes) used effectively.

Both are well-organized with clear sections moving from hook to product differentiation to social proof/trust to CTA. Draft A includes a testimonial which adds structural variety; Draft B is more uniform but tidy.

Draft A is significantly more engaging — its voice is distinctive, the italicized 'huh' moment is vivid, the testimonial adds authenticity, and the closing line is compelling. Draft B is competent but reads more like standard marketing copy without memorable hooks.
Draft A has a confident, warm, conversational voice that feels distinctive and consistent throughout. Draft B's tone is pleasant and appropriate but more generic — it could belong to many brands.

generic_judge_result.overall_reasoning: While Draft B is slightly clearer and more concise, Draft A is substantially more engaging, memorable, and distinctive in voice. Its use of a testimonial, vivid sensory details ('day three... not day thirty-seven'), and a punchy closing CTA make it the stronger piece of marketing writing overall.

writing_task: Hey, I need help with something a little different from the usual acquisition copy. We're launching a referral program for our existing customers — people who already love the brand and want to share it — and I need the landing page hero section (headline + 2-3 short paragraphs) that they land on after clicking the referral invite email. The product is a premium sleep supplement, and these are warm, loyal customers, not cold traffic, so the tone can assume some familiarity. The goal is to get them to share their unique link, but I want it to feel like we're letting them in on something, not recruiting them for an MLM.

drafts.rubric:
You've been sleeping better. We thought you should know why.

Share the sleep, keep the credit.

We don't run ads. We never have. Every person who found us found us through someone who actually uses what we make — a text, a recommendation, a "just try it." You're already part of that chain, whether you knew it or not. This is just us making it official.

Your unique link is below. When someone you share it with makes their first purchase, you both get a reward. No tiers, no hustle, no awkward pitching. You know what you've been waking up as lately. Just pass it on.

"I sent it to three people the same night I got it. I'd been trying to explain the difference for months." — Priya M., customer since 2022

Get your link →
drafts.generic:
You Sleep Well. Now Help Someone Else Do the Same.

You already know what a real night's sleep feels like. That's why we wanted you to be the first to know — we're opening up a way for you to share that with people you actually care about.

When someone you refer tries it and loves it (and they will), you both get rewarded. It's less of a program and more of a conversation you were probably already having.

Grab your unique link below. No pitching required — just share it the way you'd share anything you genuinely believe in.
drafts.preference:
You sleep better than most people. Let's keep it that way — for someone you actually like.

When you share your link, a friend gets their first order at a discount, and you get credit toward yours. Nobody's pitching anyone. You're just doing what you'd do anyway: telling someone about the thing that finally fixed your 2am wake-ups.

The people who sleep well tend to share this quietly, like a good restaurant they're not sure they want getting too popular. We get that. But if there's someone in your life who needs this, now's a good time to reach out.

user_reason: A wins because it does everything right for how I think about landing pages. The headline creates real tension — 'You've been sleeping better. We thought you should know why.' makes you need the next line, which is exactly what a headline should do. The 'we don't run ads' paragraph earns the ask by reframing the customer as already part of something, not being recruited into something. And the Priya quote lands mid-narrative right when you need social proof, not bolted on at the bottom. The CTA flows so naturally from everything above it that skipping it would feel weird. C is second because it has strong voice and the 'good restaurant' bit is genuinely good — it captures the insider feeling I wanted without being precious about it. But the headline tries to do too much and the structure feels like two separate ideas that didn't fully merge. B is the weakest. 'You Sleep Well. Now Help Someone Else Do the Same.' summarizes instead of creates tension, 'and they will' in parentheses is the kind of confident-but-hollow move I hate, and 'it's less of a program and more of a conversation' is exactly the kind of line that sounds smart but says nothing. It's the safest draft, which in this context is the same as the worst one.

criteria_analysis.name: Narrative Tension & Forward Pull
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft opens with 'You've been sleeping better. We thought you should know why.' — a genuine tension gap that implies the brand knows something the reader doesn't, pulling them forward without explaining its own device. The generic draft's headline ('You Sleep Well. Now Help Someone Else Do the Same') summarizes the entire page and removes all reason to keep reading. The original preference draft has a mildly provocative opener ('for someone you actually like') but it's more of a personality flourish than real tension — the ask is clear from sentence one.

criteria_analysis.name: Category Distinctiveness
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft's 'Share the sleep, keep the credit' subhead and the specificity about no ads/no tiers give it a distinctive voice that couldn't be dropped onto a competitor's page easily. The original preference draft achieves distinctiveness through the 'good restaurant they don't want getting too popular' analogy and '2am wake-ups' — fresh, specific framing. The generic draft reads like boilerplate referral copy any DTC brand could use; 'a real night's sleep' and 'share it with people you actually care about' are stock phrases.

criteria_analysis.name: Seamless Persuasive Thread
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft builds a single continuous argument: you've been sleeping better → here's why (community, not ads) → here's how to extend that → the quote resolves the sharing impulse → 'Get your link' feels like the only logical exhale. There's one CTA, no register shift, and the 'sleep' thread bookends the piece. The generic draft has a slight register shift at 'Grab your unique link' (suddenly more transactional) and the CTA doesn't grow from a preceding emotional beat. The original preference draft holds tone well but the three-paragraph structure feels like three adjacent ideas more than one inevitable argument — the CTA is implied but never explicitly placed, which weakens the persuasive close.

criteria_analysis.name: Benefits Grounded in Substance
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The original preference draft anchors the benefit to a specific moment ('2am wake-ups') and plainly states the mechanism (friend gets discount, you get credit) — concrete and grounded. The rubric-guided draft has 'You know what you've been waking up as lately,' which is visceral but slightly vague; it plainly states the referral mechanism but doesn't ground the sleep benefit in substance. The generic draft offers no specific moments, no mechanism, and 'a real night's sleep' is too abstract to count as grounded.

criteria_analysis.name: Integrated Social Proof
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft places Priya's quote mid-argument, after 'You know what you've been waking up as lately. Just pass it on' — the copy builds toward the sharing impulse without fully resolving it, and the quote completes the thought by showing someone who actually did it immediately. The specific detail ('three people the same night') and the time reference ('trying to explain the difference for months') give it texture. The generic draft has no social proof at all. The original preference draft also has no customer quote.

criteria_analysis.name: Quiet Confidence in Tone
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft nails the insider feeling — 'You're already part of that chain, whether you knew it or not. This is just us making it official' — it's knowing, warm, and confident without a single hype word. The original preference draft is also strong ('like a good restaurant they're not sure they want getting too popular' is perfect quiet confidence) but 'You sleep better than most people' borders on flattery that feels slightly performative. The generic draft is competent but bland — 'and they will' is the only moment of real confidence, and phrases like 'people you actually care about' feel like they're trying to earn warmth they haven't built.

rubric_judge_result.overall_reasoning: The rubric-guided draft excels across nearly every criterion this user cares most about: implicit headline tension, seamless persuasive thread, integrated social proof that completes an open thought, and quiet insider confidence — all priorities visible in their conversation history. The original preference draft has strong distinctiveness and grounded benefits but lacks social proof and a fully realized CTA structure. The generic draft reads like category-interchangeable referral copy and misses most of what this user iterates hardest on.

per_criterion.reasoning: Both are clear, but Draft B communicates the referral concept more directly and succinctly. Draft A's line 'You know what you've been waking up as lately' is slightly ambiguous.

Both drafts flow logically from establishing the reader's experience to introducing the referral program. Draft A's detour into explaining 'no ads' is interesting but slightly tangential; Draft B stays tightly on message.
Both drafts are free of grammatical errors and mechanically sound.

Draft A has a more developed structure with a clear headline, subheadline, body sections, a testimonial, and a CTA. Draft B is well-organized but more minimal, lacking the testimonial and layered structure that gives Draft A more completeness.

Draft A is more engaging — the opening hook is intriguing, the backstory about no ads adds texture, and the testimonial from Priya M. adds social proof and emotional resonance. Draft B is pleasant but less memorable.

Both have a warm, conversational tone appropriate for a referral email. Draft A's voice is more distinctive and confident — lines like 'No tiers, no hustle, no awkward pitching' have personality. Draft B's parenthetical '(and they will)' is charming but the overall voice is slightly more generic.

generic_judge_result.overall_reasoning: Draft A is the stronger piece overall, offering a more distinctive voice, richer structural elements (subheadline, backstory, testimonial, CTA), and greater engagement, despite Draft B's slight edge in directness and clarity.

writing_task: Okay so I need help with something a little different from what we've been doing. We're launching a new line of home office furniture — specifically an adjustable standing desk — and I need the **hero section copy** for the landing page. The audience here is remote workers who are already skeptical of "wellness" products because they've been burned before (bought the foam roller, never used it, you know the type). Can you write me a headline + 2-3 short paragraphs that acknowledge that skepticism without wallowing in it, and then pivot to why this desk actually changes the behavior, not just the intention?

drafts.rubric:
You already know this isn't going to work.

That's the thing about buying something for your health — the moment it arrives, you've already half-decided it's not for you. The foam roller is under the bed. The blue-light glasses are in a drawer somewhere. You bought the intention, not the habit.

This desk doesn't ask you to form a habit. It moves when you do — one touch adjusts the height, so the activation cost of standing up is basically zero. That's the actual mechanism: friction removed, not motivation added. The behavior changes because the barrier disappears.

"I stood for four hours yesterday without thinking about it once. That's never happened before." — Renata K., freelance editor, six weeks in.

The desk sits at whatever height you left it. It remembers. Turns out that's all it takes.

See it in your space →
drafts.generic:
Yeah, we know. You've got a graveyard of good intentions somewhere — the foam roller, the blue-light glasses, the standing mat still in its packaging.

This isn't another thing to feel guilty about. The Shift Desk is built around one uncomfortable truth: you won't change your habits through willpower. You'll change them when the environment makes the better choice the easier one.

A smooth, one-touch adjustment means you'll actually use it — not once, not when you remember, but automatically, the way you reach for your coffee. Less commitment. More movement. A desk that does the work of changing your day.
drafts.preference:
You already have a graveyard of good intentions in your closet. The resistance bands. The lumbar pillow. The water bottle you were definitely going to track.

This is different — and not because we're telling you it is.

The reason most "wellness" purchases fail isn't willpower. It's friction. If something takes effort to use, you won't use it. This desk moves in three seconds, fits naturally into how you already work, and makes standing the path of least resistance instead of the virtuous detour.

You don't need more motivation. You just need a setup that stops working against you.

user_reason: A wins because the headline creates real tension — 'You already know this isn't going to work' makes you need to keep reading, which is exactly what a hero headline should do. It also earns the social proof by placing Renata's quote right where it reinforces the behavior-change point, not bolted on at the end. The CTA 'See it in your space →' flows naturally from the copy instead of screaming buy now. B is solid — the friction argument is clean and the voice is right — but it never fully lands the 'so what' of how the desk actually removes friction mechanically; it tells you friction is the problem without showing you the solution as vividly as A does. C has a good instinct with the environment line but 'The Shift Desk' naming mid-hero feels like a product pitch creeping in, and 'Less commitment. More movement.' is the kind of punchy-but-vague triple that sounds like every other wellness brand in the category, which is exactly the audience's sore spot.

criteria_analysis.name: Narrative Tension & Forward Pull
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft opens with 'You already know this isn't going to work' — a provocative, implicit tension gap that makes you need to know what comes next, and it never front-loads its resolution. The generic draft opens with a decent graveyard metaphor but 'This isn't another thing to feel guilty about' resolves too quickly and feels slightly on-the-nose. The original preference draft similarly front-loads the 'graveyard' image and then explicitly announces 'This is different — and not because we're telling you it is,' which is a tension device that explains its own intent.

criteria_analysis.name: Category Distinctiveness
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft's framing around 'friction removed, not motivation added' and 'activation cost' feels genuinely distinctive — you couldn't paste this onto a competitor's page. The generic draft uses phrases like 'Less commitment. More movement' and 'A desk that does the work of changing your day' which are interchangeable category language. The original preference draft has a somewhat fresh 'path of least resistance instead of the virtuous detour' line but overall stays closer to standard wellness-product copy.

criteria_analysis.name: Seamless Persuasive Thread
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft maintains a single continuous thread — skepticism → mechanism → proof → quiet landing → CTA that feels like a natural exhale ('See it in your space →'). The 'it remembers' line sustains momentum right into the CTA without deflating. The generic draft has decent flow but ends on a generic pitch line ('A desk that does the work of changing your day') and has no CTA. The original preference draft ends on 'You don't need more motivation. You just need a setup that stops working against you' — which resolves the argument fully with no CTA, exactly the deflation pattern this user flagged in conversation.

criteria_analysis.name: Integrated Social Proof
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft builds an open thought about zero activation cost and behavior changing, then hands off to Renata's quote to complete it — 'I stood for four hours without thinking about it once' resolves the 'friction removed' claim with a specific, concrete detail. The generic and original preference drafts include no social proof at all.

criteria_analysis.name: Benefits Grounded in Substance
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft grounds its benefit in a specific mechanism ('one touch adjusts the height') and translates it immediately ('activation cost is basically zero'). The original preference draft also does this well with 'moves in three seconds' and 'path of least resistance,' though it stays slightly more abstract. The generic draft mentions 'smooth, one-touch adjustment' but the benefit translation ('the way you reach for your coffee') is more atmospheric than grounded.

criteria_analysis.name: Quiet Confidence in Tone
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft reads like a smart friend who already knows what you're thinking — 'That's the actual mechanism' and 'Turns out that's all it takes' land with understated ease. The generic draft opens with 'Yeah, we know' which is conversational but then slides into pitch-mode with 'The Shift Desk is built around one uncomfortable truth,' which feels like copy performing insight.

rubric_judge_result.overall_reasoning: The rubric-guided draft excels across nearly every criterion — its tension is implicit, its social proof is structurally integrated (not decorative), its CTA grows organically from the preceding beat, and its tone has the understated confidence this user specifically values. The original preference draft is solid on substance and tone but lacks social proof, a CTA, and its self-referential tension device ('not because we're telling you it is') is the kind of on-the-nose move the user consistently rejects. The generic draft reads like competent category copy but lacks distinctiveness, social proof, and the seamless thread this user demands.

per_criterion.reasoning: Draft A clearly articulates the core mechanism (friction removal vs. motivation) with precision. Draft B is also clear but slightly more abstract in explaining how the desk works.

Draft A builds a tight logical chain: failed intentions → why they fail → how this desk sidesteps that → proof via testimonial → simple close. Draft B follows a similar arc but the transition from the behavioral insight to the product description feels slightly less seamless.
Both drafts are mechanically clean with no grammatical errors or typos. Both use intentional fragments effectively for voice.

Draft A has a more deliberate structure — hook, explanation, testimonial, closing image, CTA — each paragraph doing distinct work. Draft B is well-organized but lacks a testimonial or closing beat that rounds it off; it ends somewhat abruptly.
Both are highly engaging. Draft A's opening line and the testimonial pull the reader in powerfully. Draft B's 'graveyard of good intentions' is equally vivid and the coffee analogy is memorable.
Both maintain a consistent, smart, conversational tone that avoids typical ad copy hype. Each feels authentic and respects the reader's intelligence.

generic_judge_result.overall_reasoning: Draft A edges ahead with a more complete structure (testimonial, memorable closing image, CTA) and a sharper articulation of the core insight ('friction removed, not motivation added'). Draft B is nearly as strong but ends less satisfyingly and explains the mechanism with slightly less precision.
